title: Lamentations 3
subtitle: His Compassions Never Fail

heb.paragraph: "His compassions never fail. They are new (ḥădāshîm) every morning; great is your faithfulness" (vv.22–23). THE centre of Lamentations and its only moment of sustained hope. The chapter is a TRIPLE acrostic (3 verses per Hebrew letter = 66 verses). It begins in darkness: "I am the man who has seen affliction by the rod of the LORD’s wrath" (v.1). Driven into darkness (v.2), flesh wasted (v.4), walled in (v.7), shut out from prayer (v.8). Then v.21: "Yet this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope." The turn is volitional — he CALLS to mind. Hope is a decision, not a feeling. vv.22–23 follow: compassions new every morning. Then the declaration: "The LORD is my portion; therefore I will wait for him" (v.24).

refs.note: Weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning — the same morning-renewal theology as Lam 3:23.
In all things God works for the good of those who love him — the NT version of Lam 3:25–26 (waiting for God’s salvation).
He who promised is faithful — the same faithfulness (ʾĕmūnâ) that Lam 3:23 celebrates.
God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever — the psalm equivalent of Lam 3:24.

notes.note: "I am the man who has seen affliction." The individual voice: one man’s suffering representing all. Driven into darkness, flesh wasted, walled in.
"He has walled me in; he has weighed me down with chains. Even when I call or cry, he shuts out my prayer." God blocking prayer is the deepest darkness: when the escape-route itself is closed.

"Yet this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope." The TURN. Volitional: he DECIDES to remember. "His compassions never fail. New every morning. Great is your faithfulness. The LORD is my portion."

"The LORD is good to those whose hope is in him. It is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD." Waiting as active faith. Bearing the yoke in youth. Sitting alone in silence when God imposes it.
Calvin calls these the most important verses in the OT for sufferers: the decision to hope is made INSIDE the darkness, not after it lifts. Hope is not the absence of grief but its companion.

Calvin reads the "waiting quietly" as the hardest spiritual discipline: not passive resignation but active trust when every circumstance contradicts the promise. To wait for God when God seems to have abandoned you is the ultimate act of faith.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that chesed (covenant love) and rachamim (compassions/womb-mercies) are paired here: God’s committed loyalty and his tender mercy together constitute the ground of hope. Neither alone would suffice.

The NET observes "the LORD is my portion" echoes the Levitical inheritance tradition (Num 18:20; Ps 16:5): the Levites received no land because God himself was their inheritance. The sufferer claims a priestly identity.

berlin.source: Adele Berlin, Lamentations, OTL (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Berlin notes the first 20 verses systematically demolish every possible ground of hope: health, freedom, prayer, memory, peace. The demolition is deliberate — it creates a void that only vv.22–23 can fill.

Berlin reads "new every morning" as a counter to the night-weeping of 1:2. If grief belongs to the night, mercy belongs to the morning. The daily renewal matches the daily destruction: grace is as persistent as suffering.

oconnor.source: Kathleen M. O'Connor, Lamentations, NCBC (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Connor reads the hope-passage as a survival strategy, not a theological conclusion: the sufferer chooses to remember mercy because the alternative is annihilation. Hope here is not optimism but the refusal to die.

O’Connor notes the ‘waiting quietly’ is not passivity but the patience of someone who has no other option. The postures — sitting alone, putting mouth in the dust, offering the cheek to the striker — are embodied submission. Jesus enacts these in the Passion (cf. Isa 50:6).

hist.context: The triple acrostic (each Hebrew letter governs three verses) makes this the most formally elaborate poem in the OT. The first 20 verses are unrelieved suffering; vv.21–33 are the hope-centre; the remainder returns to lament. The hope does NOT replace the grief but exists INSIDE it. Thomas Obadiah Chisholm wrote the hymn "Great Is Thy Faithfulness" (1923) from vv.22–23, making this the most musically influential passage in Lamentations. The "I am the man" (v.1) voice is individual, not corporate — one sufferer speaking for all.

sections.header: Verses 34–66 — “Let Us Examine Our Ways and Return to the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Let us examine (naḥpəsâ) our ways and test them, and let us return to the LORD. Let us lift up our hearts and our hands to God in heaven" (vv.40–41). The individual voice becomes communal: "let US." But confession meets silence: "We have sinned and rebelled, and you have not forgiven" (v.42). Fear and pit and snare (v.47). "Streams of tears flow from my eyes because my people are destroyed" (v.48). God hunted them like a bird (v.52). From the depths of the pit: "You came near when I called you; you said, Do not fear" (v.57). The chapter ends petitioning God to repay the enemies (vv.64–66).

refs.note: If my people humble themselves, pray, seek my face, and turn from their wicked ways — the pattern Lam 3:40–41 follows.
Out of the depths I cry to you, LORD — the same "pit" prayer as Lam 3:55.
Do not take revenge; leave room for God’s wrath — the NT version of Lam 3:64–66’s imprecation: let GOD repay.
Hard pressed but not crushed; persecuted but not abandoned — the Pauline echo of Lam 3’s survival-through-suffering.

notes.note: "Let us examine our ways and return to the LORD." The communal call. But: "We have sinned and you have NOT FORGIVEN." The unforgiveness is stated without bitterness, as fact.
"Streams of tears flow. My eyes will flow unceasingly, without relief, until the LORD looks down from heaven and sees." The tears are presented as evidence: look at these, God.
"I called your name from the depths of the pit. You came near. You said, Do not fear." A past rescue remembered. Even in the pit, God once spoke. The memory sustains.
"Pay them back for what they have done. Put a veil over their hearts." The imprecation: hand the enemies to God. Trust his justice rather than enacting your own.

Calvin notes the honest juxtaposition: "let us return" (v.40) then "you have not forgiven" (v.42). Repentance does not guarantee immediate relief. Calvin calls this the mature faith that persists beyond felt forgiveness.

Calvin reads the remembered deliverance as the bridge between past grace and future hope: God spoke "Do not fear" once; he can speak it again. Memory of past faithfulness is fuel for present endurance.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the shocking frankness: "you have not forgiven." The Hebrew lo salahta is a direct statement of God’s ongoing wrath. The poet does not sugarcoat the situation even after the hope of vv.22–23.
The NET observes a shift to past tense: "you came near," "you said." This is remembered deliverance, not current experience. The gap between past rescue and present suffering is the space of faith.

berlin.source: Adele Berlin, Lamentations, OTL (2002) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Berlin reads the communal shift ("let US") as the poem’s attempt to move from individual to collective processing. Grief shared is grief survived.
Berlin notes the "Do not fear" is the only reported divine speech in all five poems. God speaks exactly once — and what he says is: do not be afraid. The rarity makes the word precious.

oconnor.source: Kathleen M. O'Connor, Lamentations, NCBC (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Connor reads ‘you have not forgiven’ as the most courageous sentence in the book: after confessing sin, after hoping in mercy, the poet reports that forgiveness has not arrived. The honesty is brutal and essential.

O’Connor notes the chapter ends with imprecation, not resolution. The sufferer hands the enemy to God’s judgment. This is not vengeful but realistic: someone must answer for what happened. Entrusting that answer to God is an act of trust, not hate.

hist.context: The second half of chapter 3 moves through three movements: communal confession (vv.40–42), resumed individual lament (vv.43–54), and recalled deliverance (vv.55–58) followed by a plea for justice (vv.59–66). The admission "you have not forgiven" (v.42) is extraordinary — it comes AFTER the hope of vv.22–23. Hope does not guarantee immediate resolution. The closing imprecation (vv.64–66) may trouble modern readers, but it channels rage toward God rather than toward vigilante action. The prayer for vengeance IS the restraint: it trusts God to act rather than acting oneself.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: " "The LORD is good to those whose hope is in him…

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: 22–23, making this the most musically influential passage in Lamentations…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI am the man who has seen affliction by the rod of the Lord\u2019s wrath.ESVI am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of his wrath;NIVHe has driven me away and made me walk in darkness rather than light;ESVhe has driven and brought me into darkness without any light;

src.title: Sumerian City Laments (c.2000 BC)
src.quote: The Lamentation over the Destruction of Ur and similar Sumerian texts follow a comparable pattern: the deity abandons the city, enemies destroy it, the population mourns, and a tentative plea for restoration concludes.
src.note: Lamentations participates in an ancient Near Eastern genre of city-lament that predates it by over a millennium. The genre validates public grief as a literary and theological act.

src.title: The Babylonian Chronicles
src.quote: Cuneiform records document Nebuchadnezzar’s siege and capture of Jerusalem in 597 and 586 BC, confirming the historical catastrophe behind Lamentations.
src.note: The archaeological evidence corroborates the destruction described in the poems, including evidence of massive burning in the City of David excavations.

rec.text: ""I am the man who has seen affliction." The individual voice: one man’s suffering representing all. Driven into darkness, flesh wasted, walled in."

rows.label: Examine and Return — 3:34–66
rows.text: "Let us EXAMINE our ways." "You have NOT FORGIVEN." "Do not fear." Imprecation entrusted to God.

Then the declaration: "The LORD is my portion; therefore I will wait for him" (v.24).

"Let us examine (naḥpəsâ) our ways and test them, and let us return to the LORD. Let us lift up our hearts and our hands to God in heaven" (vv.40–41). The individual voice becomes communal: "let US." But confession meets silence: "We have sinned and rebelled, and you have not forgiven" (v.42). Fear and pit and snare (v.47). themes.note: Mercy peaks at 10: the hope centre of the entire book. "Great is your faithfulness." Faith at 9: hope as volitional decision inside darkness.

coaching.tip: 'Great is your faithfulness' (v. 23) — the line that inspired the famous hymn — appears in the darkest chapter of the darkest book. The confession of God's faithfulness rises not from prosperity but from the pit. The poet has described being hunted, walled in, chained, and mocked. Then, at the absolute bottom, he remembers. Hope in Lamentations isn't the absence of despair — it's the refusal to let despair have the last word.

Chapter 3 is the structural and theological center of Lamentations — the third of five poems, the only one written in first person singular, and the one containing the book's only extended hope. The architecture is deliberate: grief surrounds hope on every side, yet hope holds the center. The literary structure mirrors the theological claim: affliction is the context; faithfulness is the core.

prayer_prompt: Because of the LORD's great love we are not consumed, for His compassions never fail. Consider asking God about this pivot from despair to hope — what it takes to say this in the middle of ruin.
